input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI ContentPage called SettingsPage.xaml that currently uses
plain bindings with no x:DataType. The page's BindingContext is a
SettingsViewModel with properties: string Theme, bool NotificationsEnabled,
and int FontSize. Convert this page to use compiled bindings and tell me
how to catch binding errors at build time.

data.content: No files exist in the working directory, so I'll create a complete example showing the conversion and explain how to catch errors at build time.

arguments.file_text:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8" ?>
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             xmlns:viewmodels="clr-namespace:MyApp.ViewModels"
             x:Class="MyApp.Views.SettingsPage"
             x:DataType="viewmodels:SettingsViewModel"
             Title="Settings">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="15">

        <!-- Theme (string) -->
        <Label Text="Theme" FontAttributes="Bold" />
        <Entry Text="{Binding Theme}" />

        <!-- Notifications (bool) -->
        <Label Text="Notifications" FontAttributes="Bold" />
        <Switch IsToggled="{Binding NotificationsEnabled}" />

        <!-- Font Size (int) -->
        <Label Text="{Binding FontSize, StringFormat='Font Size: {0}'}" />
        <Stepper Value="{Binding FontSize}" Minimum="8" Maximum="48" Increment="1" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>

data.reasoningText:
The directory is empty and there are no existing files. The user is asking me to help them convert a XAML page conceptually. Let me provide the guidance directly since there's no existing code to modify.

I'll explain:
1. How to add `x:DataType` to the page
2. How compiled bindings work
3. How to catch errors at build time

</ContentPage>

data.content:
Here's what changed and how it works:

### The key addition: `x:DataType`

```xml
xmlns:viewmodels="clr-namespace:MyApp.ViewModels"
x:DataType="viewmodels:SettingsViewModel"
```

These two lines are the entire conversion. `x:DataType` tells the XAML compiler the type of the `BindingContext`, so every `{Binding …}` on the page is **resolved at compile time** instead of at runtime via reflection.

### Catching binding errors at build time

1. **They're already caught.** With `x:DataType` in place, misspelling a property (e.g., `{Binding Themee}`) produces a **build error**, not a silent runtime failure.

2. **Enable warnings-as-errors** for extra safety in your `.csproj`:
   ```xml
   <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
   ```

3. **If you need to opt out** for a specific binding (e.g., binding to a different type in a `DataTemplate`), use:
   ```xml
   x:DataType="x:Null"
   ```
   This reverts that subtree to classic reflection-based binding.

### Performance bonus

Compiled bindings skip `System.Reflection` at runtime, so they're measurably faster — especially in list views with many items.
